speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEP]

speaker: interviewer
text: We're on. This is tape number three with Sandor Klein. 1944, May 4, day of the liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945. Yeah, we were liberated by the American Army and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you standing? What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I mentioned it before, that we were standing in a corner of a barrack, and saying our Friday-night prayers. And that's when the first soldier came in. And one came in a American uniform. I remember he had a white glove on, and this light beige uniform, and the army cap, put to the side. He was smiling. And suddenly, he was speaking Hungarian. We don't know what's his name was. We couldn't get to him. He said that from now on, you are free.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Shh, shh.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you are free. All of us? Yes, all of us. And where are the Germans? He said, you don't have to worry about the German soldiers. We are taking care of them. Just go back to your barrack, sit down like we are not here. Just be happy that the Germans are not here. And wait till next morning. And then we will tell you-- we will give you further instructions. I don't think there was one person who slept that night. We were just talking. We didn't know what to talk about it, talk the past or talk the future. We knew the past. That was very sad and very bad. And we didn't know what the future is. We still knew-- hear that the war is on. The army group which came to us, they were just a occupation unit, or whatever you call it. They were-- [? passed by us. ?] [? This ?] same Hungarian-speaking soldier came and explained to us that we had to go in our barracks at night because they were rounding up the Germans within to camp and outside the camp. And he explained to us that all the drums we saw when we came into the lager, the camp, were full of gasoline. And they were supposed to blow up and burn, completely down, the camp before they will leave the camp. That was the order for the German soldiers. But our chief in the lager, the German army men, had different idea. He went on a little German-- they called it the Tiger, which are going through water and dry land, wherever. It's a very small, open-top tank. He went ahead of the American Army with a big white flag, or a sheet, or whatever he had. And then he ask clemency from the army, the American Army men who met him on the road, that he will not go through with the burning down, or the blowing up the camp if himself and his family get the freedom and they can go wherever they want. Naturally, the-- the army, the American Army, gave him this. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They promised him that you will go free. Just go back to the camp. Round up your soldiers. And just give yourself up. You will go free. You will not get harmed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he told us this story also. He wished us good luck. And he even, was funny.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, now I had the chance to use my mother's language. I spoke to you and now I have other duties to do. I am leaving you. You are free to go. But he warned us, don't go close to the railroad. Because it's full of food and that's-- the-- the wagons are-- are full of weapons, German weapons. Naturally, you cannot tell this to a hungry man. And by the hoard, all who could walk ran to the railroad. And they-- they found barrels full of lard and sugar and salt. And they started to eat the lard and the sugar. I was in the camp three more days. I met, somehow, my cousin from Hungary, who was a doctor. And he told me, Sandor, you stay here, put. We will wait out until the noise go away. I will get a car and we will go home. So I was sitting and waiting. And since he was a doctor, he saw people. And he told me that they were dying, faster than they died in the gas chamber from the lard and the sugar, what they consumed with empty stomach and weakened body. And a couple days later, I told him, I don't feel good. He said, right out here from the camp is the city of Wels. It's a very short walking distance. There was the [NON-ENGLISH] that was occupied by the German Army. They will turn this into a hospital. And they have an English part, a German part, and they have a Hungarian part. But he said, I will take you in, and I will ask another doctor to check you out. I went in. The Hungarian part was full. And since I spoke a little German, no English, I was put in the German part. And when a German doctor examine me, he told me that I have the typhus. And he told me, you will have very high fever. And that was the last word I remember. I fell into a coma. And from this coma, either you come out, or you don't come out at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was lucky. 21 days, I was in that hospital with high fever, and I came out. And my first word was, where was my cousin, the doctor? And they told me, he died.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had lots of aspirin with himself. And since he had the fever, but he took the aspirin, and the fever didn't come out in him. And he was killed. We were in the [NON-ENGLISH]. I was very weak. I was about 40 kilogram, just close to 80 pound. And I got food. I don't know what kind of food it was. Hungarian, English, German, it didn't make any different. I felt much better, day in, day out. And early part of August, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they said that they have to clean out the [NON-ENGLISH]. We went into the office. And we got the paper that we are DPs, Displaced Persons. This paper was filled out in three different languages, English, German, and French. And it was signed by [? Auspitz. ?] The man's name was [? Auspitz. ?] He signed the paper and he said, you are free, where to go. We started to talk, which way to go. Some people wanted to come. At night I-- I still remember my father, last word before he went into the armed service a second times. No matter what happens, let's meet here, where we are now. That was the reason I decided that I will go back to Hungary. In the [NON-ENGLISH], before we left, I picked up some new German boots, German Army uniform, and a coat. And I almost look good in that. I started to march. We went into houses that was free, nobody occupied the houses. I was still in a little disturbance from the typhus. I collected combs and soaps. I don't know why. But I had a bread sack, full of combs and soap. My first suffering after the freedom was when we arrived to the first city then, after we left Wels. That was occupied by the Russian Army. And the Russian soldiers took everything away from me, even-- he pulled off the boot from my leg.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, what is the freedom? I am now worse than I was before. And somehow, somebody came with a Russian soldier.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not a-- not a soldier. He had some rank. I don't know what was his rank. He had a hard-head hat. And he spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never learned to speak Yiddish. But with my German, I could understand what he was asking. He ask us, are you Jewish? We said yes. Can you say some prayer? Naturally.

speaker: interviewee
text: First prayer came to mind, the Shema Yisrael. And he said, OK. You go to this room, you will get some clothing. We got clothing. And then we were told, go out to the railroad station. You can go home to Hungary. That's how I did. I arrived August 11, 1945. I arrived to Budapest. And I decided that I never will go out to Ujpest, or never will go out to the town I was born to, Kistarcsa. I will try to stay here, in Budapest, and wait out until we meet. I went to the Rabbi seminarium, where I knew my brother used to work-- go-- go to school. And that was closed through the High Holidays. And I met my brother. My brother came home earlier. He was also in Austria, not in a concentration camp. He came home from there. So we were, already, two of us. We went through the first two days of the holidays in 1945, and came the day of Yom Kippur. We went over there, to the seminarium. They had their own synagogue. We had the services-- [CRYING] And my-- my-- my father walked in. [CRYING] I'm sorry, that came out strong of me. So we all were together. And then we started another life, the three of us, together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We find out two days later, from a friend of mine who went out to Auschwitz, 1944, July the 8th. The same call-- cattle wagon where my mother was. They went out together. And she was over there when my-- when my mother was separated from the group and went straight to the gas chamber. [CRYING] She was 44 years old. [CRYING] And I tell you the truth. Since then, I never cried. I thought that I cried out all my tears. But this is-- this time is-- it's coming on too strong. I'm sorry, but-- I am almost too old to cry. But it just came out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was really the story, the story of mine. And then we started to make another life. I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working in the city of Budapest. And for the vacation, we got reward from the union. And I was sent to a-- a place where they had a building. I went there for my vacation. And on the train I met a young woman. And she became my wife later on. She came from another part of Hungary, where she was living, and after the war, she went out. Then we met and we talked. And our life almost went on the same line. She was in Mauthausen. She went through the death march. She was in Gunskirchen. We never met over there. And that's happened seven years later. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 1952-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --we got married. We have lots of thing to talk about it, in the past. She lost family. I lost lots-- lots and lots of people from my family, especially from my mother's side, who I didn't have very close contact. But they all suffered and they died. And many of them suffered in the ghetto in Budapest. Because Budapest was the last bastion. The German couldn't put their hand on it. They were not put out-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --to-- to the Auschwitz gas chamber. And from this mutual friend, I-- I-- I heard all the story about my mother. Since I mentioned it before, that I saw her last time, it was in 1944, May. They were together in this building, the ghetto. And when the time-- time came, they all had to march out to the railroad station. They were put in the wagon. According to her, they were more than 70. And she was a young person that time. She went up to the wagon, almost last. After her came in the water-- bucket of water, and the empty bucket which they used, whoever used it. And they were about four, five days on this road, going from city to city. The door never were open. And that was hot, in July. And finally, they arrived over there. And they saw the sign, 'Arbeit Macht Frei.' They march through this gate. And in the same day, in the afternoon, my mother marched into the gas chamber and went through the chimney. And this young woman came home and she told me all these stories. And next year, when the memorial wall was put up in the town of Ujpest, in the yard of the synagogue, we went out. We thought that we are free person, and there will be no more word against the Jew. As I mentioned, that the synagogue, the Conservative synagogue, was in the same street where I grew up. We walked through the street. In the building we used to live, people were standing in the window, and looking down the street. And I hear, from the same building where l live, somebody says, look at this. More of them came back than left. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was the last word which I kept in my mind until today, when I hear that more came back than left. If they would know, if they would be there at the march, if they would see the gas chamber inside, they would see the ovens. And they see the mound and mounds and mounds of dead body. Which were naturally opened out after the concentration camp was-- in the four days I spend after the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --liberation over there. The German soldiers who were rounded up, they had to open out these mass graves. And they put body parts-- there were no whole body. You know, the lye melts the body [? of you ?] very fast. They were put in 60 kilogram in each potato sack. And that is a cemetery still, in the Gunskirchen forest. I will jump now, a little bit. Life started and life went on. We had our only child, born. He grew up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Steven. In Hungarian he was [? Pishdar, ?] And he was about 3 and 1/2 years old when we had the Hungarian Revolution. The 1956 Revolution.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we talk about that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what happened to your best friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until I left Hungary in 1956, I had contact with him once or twice. He even came up to see our son when he was born. And my best knowledge, he was working as a weaver also.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I talk to him, that what is your future here? He said, I don't know. I will just work. I ask him about what did he do in the war time? And he said that he joined through to the Hungarian Arrow-- Arrow fascist, that was-- they were-- they didn't have the-- the swastika like-- like Hitler had.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a cross and the points, they had arrows. He joined the group for fun. That's-- that was his word, I thought we will have some fun over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, and what did you do for fun? He said, we round up the Jews in the city of Budapest and Ujpest, wherever we were. And we chased them down.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chased them down to the Danube. And we shot them with machine gun. And they were falling into the Blue Danube, which turned into red. That was the fun. He was 18-- 17 years old. That's what he did. That was fun, killing other people. And then he begged me, that I hope you won't-- you won't report me. You won't put me in [? nowhere. ?] I said, no. Our best time together was much stronger than this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told myself, will it be a revenge to put one these young men, who really did it for fun? Because his mind was not up to date at that time when he did what is he doing. He just followed the herd.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was one of them. He was handed a machine gun. I don't know he pulled the trigger.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he strong enough? Did he know what he is doing? I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I remember when I pulled, the first time, the trigger in the army, what kind of feeling it gave me. But I didn't shoot any person. I just shoot blank. But hit with the live bullet into a person who a couple of months before was a happy family person, and shot it in the Danube. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He said, I'm sorry what I did, but I did it for fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do--

speaker: interviewee
text: Three of them, I knew from the same house, where it was a big apartment building, that they did this. One of them went to a-- a-- in front of the judge. And he was put for, I don't know how long, two or three years, [? down ?] [? in the-- ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No I wasn't there. Not this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you participate in any of these trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was at the trials of the Hungarian fascist government, person who were found in the Austria, hiding, and they were brought back to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what capacity were you at the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was just a-- a person in the courtroom. There was no-- no witnesses of any of us. There was no deposition, what we had to do. We were just allowed to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we applied for the permit to go to the trial and listen to this, that they ask, were you somewhere in the concentration camp? Yes, I said. I was in Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said that these people were found in Austria near Salzburg and Graz, in Austria. That's where they found them. And they had a trial. Short, very fast. Much shorter than the Simpson trial. Two days, three days most. And they were found guilty. And I was there personally, where they were executed. Many of them were shot. Some of them were hung. And one or two of them committed suicide between the-- the room, the trial room, and the cell. They ran out in the hallway and jumped out of the window. But all 11 of them were executed. It was in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That didn't affect me. That time, I was completely out of myself. I was a piece of ice. Dead body, happy person, did not affect me at-- affected me at all. Even nowadays, this part in me is so strong, that if I see a dead bird, or I see an accident, it does not disturb me at all. Mentally, and-- and I'm just-- just a blank. Like-- like a shade I pull down, I close out. I just see these dead body, but it does not register that it's a dead body who was alive before, a couple of minutes before. It's just doesn't bother me. Every time I see a dead body-- I saw many of them, believe me, in my life-- this picture comes back. The dead body, one on top of the other. You hear, still hear the voice. Some of them were not 100% dead, crying, screaming, the blood going out of the body wherever they were hit by the bullet. This picture comes back. And that somehow makes me so stiff inside that I have no feeling. You know, I-- I-- I amazed that I just cried couple minutes ago. I-- I never thought that I ever will cry in my life. But that was the first time, all the emotion-- I never talked that long about my story. But when this came back--

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back just for a moment to the Mauthausen camp. When they give out the working assignment. Many of the people were working at the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how do you call, the place where they cut out stones? Some kind of pit. One is very close to-- The quarry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quarry. That's where they were working, cutting out big squares of heavy stones. And another person carried a back holder on his back-- it was made out of wood-- shaped like an L, a long piece on the back. And the flat part, another one put up the stone. And they had to go and build stairs from the quarry all the way up to the camp. I think it's 163 steps, if I remember correctly. Because we saw this place in 1970, when we went back to the Mauthausen camp site, visited. Our son, which was-- at that time he was already 16, 17 years old. He wanted to see what we wet through and where.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went over there. And we saw these steps. And we even walked up the steps.

speaker: interviewee
text: There is a-- a-- not statue. There is a sign. It's marked that these steps, I think 163 steps, were built by the people who were incarcerated or what, here in the camp. And in the same camp, at night, since I was working early morning till noontime, I didn't see this at that time. But buddies who were there told me this later on. There was the so-called death march in the camp. The camp was built on the top. And the quarry was down there. And they had to walk, every night. They had counting in the morning, early morning hours, and counting at night. After the night counting, they have to go out and walk, right at the edge, one by one, keeping one-meter distance from person to person. And the German SS soldier was standing over there with a bloodhound dog, a huge German shepherd. And he told the-- the dog-- the soldier told the dog, go ahead. And the dog pushed the men down. Alive from the top into the quarry, and died down over there. And there was another group who picked up these bodies and deliver to us for-- for burning--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a good time to stop.
